quote: His squire was not bald by chance. Egg had the purple eyes of old Valyria, and hair that shone like beaten gold and strands of silver woven together.
why: Dunk's private thought naming the Targaryen features (purple eyes, gold/silver hair) Egg's shaved head is meant to hide.

quote: "Well," said Egg, "we could use my boot to get across."
why: Egg's concealed "boot" (a token of rank/means) offered as a shortcut, immediately shut down by Dunk as too dangerous.

quote: The grave has claimed them, every one, yet he endures, this pale bird with bloody beak who perches on King Aerys's shoulder and caws into his ear.
why: The hunchbacked septon's treasonous sermon names Bloodraven as Aerys I's ever-present Hand.

quote: A shadow came at his command to strangle brave Prince Valarr's sons in their mother's womb.
why: The septon's public accusation that Bloodraven used sorcery to kill Valarr's unborn sons.

quote: Rise up, I say, and remember our true king across the water. Seven gods there are, and seven kingdoms, and the Black Dragon sired seven sons!
why: The septon's treasonous call to remember the Black Dragon (Daemon I Blackfyre) as the true king across the water.

quote: His revolt had ended on the Redgrass Field, where Daemon and his twin sons died beneath a rain of Lord Bloodraven's arrows. Those rebels who survived and bent the knee were pardoned, but some lost land, some titles, some gold.
why: Narration summarizing the First Blackfyre Rebellion's end and the terms given to surviving rebels.

quote: "Only on his shield, ser. House Peake did hold three castles once, but two of them were lost."
why: Egg explains House Peake's reduced arms as the visible mark of Gormon Peake's Blackfyre attainder.

quote: I am a vagabond hedge knight like yourself. Ser John the Fiddler, I am called.
why: The mystery hedge knight's cover introduction as "Ser John the Fiddler" -- later revealed as Daemon Blackfyre II.

quote: "A slice off the roast?" the boy said. "A bit of duck, a bowl of stew? Whatever they have, ser."
why: Egg's hungry wish-list of inn fare after days of hard salt beef on the road.

quote: "Well met, ser. Come, ride with us to Whitewalls and break a few lances to help Lord Butterwell celebrate his new marriage. I'll wager you could give a good account of yourself."
why: The Fiddler's invitation drawing Dunk and Egg toward the Whitewalls wedding tourney.

quote: "Perchance we'll meet again someday. I hope so. I should love to try my lance on you."
why: The Fiddler's recurring, foreshadowing wish to joust Dunk.

quote: "A short one, ser." Egg knew better than to admit that Egg was short for Aegon. Not to men he did not know.
why: Narration confirming Egg's name is short for Aegon, and that he conceals this from strangers.

quote: "You're Fireball's son," Egg said.
why: Egg's in-text identification of Ser Glendon Ball as Fireball's (Quentyn Ball's) son.

quote: Aerys is weak, and when he dies, it will be bloody war between Lord Rivers and Prince Maekar for the crown, the Hand against the heir.
why: Kyle the Cat names Bloodraven as "Lord Rivers," the King's Hand, and frames the coming succession war.

quote: "King Aegon presented the egg to his father's father after guesting for a night at his old castle," said Ser Maynard Plumm.
why: Plumm relates how the dragon's egg -- the tourney prize -- came into House Butterwell's keeping.

quote: Aegon the Unworthy had bedded half the maidens in the realm and fathered bastards on the lot of them, supposedly. Worse, the old king had legitimized them all upon his deathbed; the baseborn ones born of tavern wenches, whores, and shepherd girls, and the Great Bastards whose mothers had been highborn.
why: Origin of the Great Bastards: Aegon IV's deathbed legitimization of his many bastard children.

quote: "Daemon Blackfyre was no traitor. The old king gave him the sword. He saw the worthiness in Daemon, even though he was born bastard. Why else would he put Blackfyre into his hand in place of Daeron's?
why: Ser Glendon's defense of Daemon Blackfyre, citing Aegon IV's gift of the ancestral sword Blackfyre over his trueborn heir Daeron.

quote: When the Black Dragon rose, this lord of cows sent one son to Daemon and one to Daeron, to make certain there was a Butterwell on the winning side. Both perished on the Redgrass Field, and his youngest died in the spring.
why: House Butterwell hedged both sides of the First Blackfyre Rebellion and lost sons on both.

quote: "His eye is fixed on Tyrosh, where Bittersteel sits in exile, plotting with the sons of Daemon Blackfyre. So he keeps the king's ships close at hand, lest they attempt to cross."
why: Ser Maynard describes Bittersteel's ongoing exile plotting and Bloodraven's watch on the crossing.

quote: Bloodraven put King Aerys on the Iron Throne, but for how long?
why: Kyle the Cat states plainly that Bloodraven made Aerys I king, and questions how long that arrangement can hold.

quote: "It's wild boar," the woman said, "well peppered, and served with onions, mushrooms, and mashed neeps."
why: The inn's roast boar, reserved for lordly guests, contrasted with the hedge knights' hard salt beef.

quote: "Ser Maynard has a bag of apples," said Kyle the Cat. "And I have pickled eggs and onions. Why, together we have the makings of a feast!
why: Kyle the Cat welcomes Dunk and Egg by pooling the hedge knights' food into a shared campfire meal.

quote: May we share your fire?"
why: Dunk's open request to share the hedge knights' fire -- guest-fire courtesy offered by a stranger on the road.

quote: Before long the trees opened up, and they found themselves in what must once have been a weirwood grove. Only a ring of white stumps and a tangle of bone-pale roots remained to show where the trees had stood, when the children of the forest ruled in Westeros.
why: The felled weirwood grove used as a campsite, with narration reaching back to the children of the forest's era.

quote: "The dragon's egg? Is that the champion's prize? Truly?" The last dragon had perished half a century ago.
why: Dunk and Egg's astonished reaction on learning the dragon's egg is the tourney's champion prize.

quote: Dunk was so used to Egg that sometimes he forgot Aegon was a prince. Of course they'd put a dragon egg inside his cradle.
why: Narration confirms Egg is a Targaryen prince who was cradled with a dragon's egg at birth.

quote: "I give you the King's Hand, Brynden Rivers. May the Crone's lamp light his path to wisdom."
why: Lord Frey's wedding toast names Bloodraven by his birth name, Brynden Rivers, and his office, the King's Hand.

quote: Young Ser Glendon had a harder time, however. "Fireball never had a son," Dunk heard Lord Butterwell's steward tell him, loudly.
why: Butterwell's steward publicly disputes Ser Glendon's claim to be Fireball's son.

quote: I can make sweet song with either lance or resined bow, as it happens. Every wedding needs a singer, and every tourney needs a mystery knight. May I join you?
why: The Fiddler frames himself as the tourney's mystery knight, hinting he already knows more than he lets on.

quote: "I'd show you mine, ser, but it's at Summerhall."
why: Egg's aside that he too owns a dragon's egg, kept at Summerhall.

quote: "I'm not stupid, ser." Egg lowered his voice. "Someday the dragons will return. My brother Daeron's dreamed of it, and King Aerys read it in a prophecy.
why: Egg cites his brother Daeron's dragon-return dream and King Aerys's own prophecy reading.

quote: Ser Quentyn Ball was the master-at-arms at the Red Keep. He taught my father and my uncles how to fight. The Great Bastards too.
why: Fireball's role training the Targaryen princes and the Great Bastards at the Red Keep.

quote: King Aegon promised to raise him to the Kingsguard, so Fireball made his wife join the silent sisters, only by the time a place came open, King Aegon was dead and King Daeron named Ser Willam Wylde instead.
why: Fireball set aside his wife for a promised Kingsguard place that went instead to Ser Willam Wylde after Aegon IV's death.

quote: "Born bastard," Ser Uthor agreed mildly, "but his royal father made him legitimate as he lay dying."
why: Ser Uthor confirms Bloodraven's legitimization by Aegon IV on his deathbed.

quote: How many eyes does Lord Bloodraven have? the riddle went. A thousand eyes, and one.
why: The Bloodraven eyes-riddle recalled again at the Whitewalls feast.

quote: "No, the old Grey Lion, the one who died in the spring." That was how men spoke of those who had perished during the Great Spring Sickness. He died in the spring. Tens of thousands had died in the spring, among them a king and two young princes.
why: The Great Spring Sickness's death toll, including a king and two princes, entered common speech as "died in the spring."

quote: Though uninvited, the three of them had been welcomed to the feast quick enough; it was ill luck to refuse a knight hospitality on your wedding day.
why: The custom that a wedding host must not turn away a knight, even uninvited hedge knights.

quote: Suckling pig was served at the high table, a peacock roasted in its plumage, a great pike crusted with crushed almonds. Not a bite of that made it down below the salt. Instead of suckling pig, they got salt pork, soaked in almond milk and peppered pleasantly.
why: Below-the-salt fare (salt pork) contrasted with the high table's suckling pig, peacock, and pike.

quote: When they cut it open, half a hundred birds burst forth to fly around the hall. In other wedding feasts Dunk had attended, the pies had been filled with doves or songbirds, but inside this one were bluejays and skylarks, pigeons and doves, mockingbirds and nightingales, small brown sparrows and a great red parrot.
why: The wedding pie cut open to release one-and-twenty sorts of live birds, pigeons among them.

quote: Whitewalls was almost new as castles went, having been raised a mere forty years ago by the grandsire of its present lord. The smallfolk hereabouts called it the Milk house, for its walls and keeps and towers were made of finely dressed white stone, quarried in the Vale and brought over the mountains at great expense. Inside were floors and pillars of milky white marble veined with gold; the rafters overhead were carved from the bone-pale trunks of weirwoods.
why: Description of Whitewalls castle: white stone walls, the "Milk house" nickname, and weirwood rafters.

quote: Ser Uthor chuckled. "There's just the joust, I fear, but besides the dragon's egg, Lord Butterwell has promised thirty golden dragons for the loser of the final tilt, and ten each for the knights defeated in the round before."
why: The tourney's stakes -- the dragon's egg plus gold prizes -- draw Dunk toward entering.

quote: "A dragon would. The prince insists the egg will hatch. He dreamed it, just as he once dreamed his brothers dead. A living dragon will win us all the swords that we would want."
why: A conspirator relays that "the prince" (Daemon II) insists his dragon-hatching dream is true, just as his earlier dream of his brothers' deaths came true.

quote: "Just do your part as promised, and let me concern myself with that. Once we have Butterwell's gold and the swords of House Frey, Harrenhal will follow, then the Brackens. Otho knows he cannot hope to stand ..."
why: Lord Peake's plan to build the rebellion's muster house by house, starting with Butterwell and Frey.

quote: "Bittersteel be buggered," insisted a familiar voice. "No bastard can be trusted, not even him. A few victories will bring him over the water fast enough."
why: A conspirator's dismissive confidence that early victories will draw Bittersteel back from exile.

quote: I suppose that means I'll have to take the throne, then. I would much rather be teaching you to fiddle.
why: The Fiddler's rueful admission that he means to claim the Iron Throne.

quote: I dreamed it. This pale white castle, you, a dragon bursting from an egg, I dreamed it all, just as I once dreamed of my brothers lying dead. They were twelve and I was only seven, so they laughed at me, and died. I am two-and-twenty now, and I trust my dreams.
why: The Fiddler (Daemon II) links his boyhood dream of his brothers' deaths to his present dream of Whitewalls and the dragon's egg.

quote: "Why," the Fiddler said, "I dreamed that you were all in white from head to heel, with a long pale cloak flowing from those broad shoulders. You were a White Sword, ser, a Sworn Brother of the Kingsguard, the greatest knight in all the Seven Kingdoms, and you lived for no other purpose but to guard and serve and please your king." He put a hand on Dunk's shoulder.
why: The Fiddler's true-dream of Dunk clad in Kingsguard white, told to Dunk directly.

quote: "To keep my hair shaved or dyed," the boy said with obvious reluctance, " and tell no man my true name."
why: Egg recites his father Prince Maekar's charge to conceal his identity.

quote: The whole realm knew how Prince Maekar's mace had felled his brother Baelor Breakspear at Ashford Meadow. Talk of plots was only to be expected.
why: The realm's living memory of Baelor Breakspear's death, invoked amid talk of new plots.

quote: I dreamed of you and a dead dragon, Egg's brother Daeron said to him. A great beast, huge, with wings so large, they could cover this meadow. It had fallen on top of you, but you were alive and the dragon was dead.
why: Dunk recalls Daeron's earlier true-dream of him and a dead dragon (Baelor Breakspear).

quote: The scales were smooth beneath his fingers, and the deep, rich red seemed to shimmer as he turned the egg in his hands. Blood and flame, he thought, but there were gold flecks in it as well, and whorls of midnight black.
why: Dunk's close description of Butterwell's dragon's egg, the tourney prize the conspirators covet.

quote: Before Dunk quite realized what was happening, John the Fiddler had dragged him to his feet. "Here!" he cried out. "Let the giant carry her!"
why: The bedding-ceremony horseplay at the Whitewalls wedding feast, with Dunk dragooned into carrying the bride.

quote: His Lordship will not be the first to butter that biscuit. His bride was deflowered by a scullion at the Twins, they say. She would creep down to the kitchens to meet him.
why: The rumor of the Frey bride's ruin that supposedly forced the Butterwell match.

quote: All the while the wine kept flowing. The rich Arbor reds gave way to local vintages, or so the Fiddler said; if truth be told, Dunk could not tell the difference. There was hippocras as well, he had to try a cup of that.
why: The wedding-night wines served at Whitewalls, ending in hippocras.

quote: "They fed us ribs, ser." Egg's nose was deep in the book. "Lord Sunderland fought for the Black Dragon, ser."
why: Egg notes that Lord Sunderland -- present at the wedding -- fought for the Black Dragon in the First Blackfyre Rebellion.

quote: "I am Glendon Ball, not Glendon Flowers. Mock me at your peril, herald. I warn you, I have hero's blood."
why: Ser Glendon publicly rejects the bastard name "Flowers," insisting on his claimed true name, Ball.

quote: His foe was young Ser Glendon, mounted on his wretched stot and armored in a heavy mail hauberk and open-faced iron halfhelm. On his arm his shield displayed his father's fiery sigil.
why: Ser Glendon rides into the lists bearing his claimed father Fireball's fiery sigil.

quote: He refuses to take ransoms. He wants no more than the dragon's egg, he says ... that, and the friendship of his fallen foes.
why: The Fiddler's chivalric refusal of ransoms as he pursues only the dragon's egg prize.

quote: The Fiddler had dismounted, to help his fallen foe back to his feet. He is one step closer to his dragon's egg, Dunk thought, and where am I?
why: Dunk's envious tally of the Fiddler's advance toward the egg he dreamed would hatch.

quote: "I know that eggs do well to stay out of frying pans," said Plumm. "Whitewalls is not a healthy place for the boy."
why: Maynard Plumm's veiled warning shows he knows more about Egg's true importance than he lets on.

quote: "So do I," said Maynard Plumm. "A bad feeling, for any man or boy unwise enough to stand in our Fiddler's way."
why: Plumm's "bad feeling" about the Fiddler, echoing the Fiddler's own prophetic dreams.

quote: The weight of the kite shield was reassuring, though its length made it awkward to handle, and seeing the hanged man once again gave him an uneasy feeling. Those are ill-omened arms.
why: Dunk's unease at his borrowed hanged-man shield, the source of his "Gallows Knight" persona at the tourney.

quote: "Would you rather die with honor intact, or live with it besmirched? No, spare me, I know what you will say. Take your boy and flee, gallows knight. Before your arms become your destiny."
why: Plumm's warning to Dunk, punning on his borrowed hanged-man ("gallows knight") arms.

quote: --the Pretender. Daemon Blackfyre struck his own coinage during his rebellion.
why: A Blackfyre gold coin, struck during Daemon I's rebellion, surfaces as "traitor's gold" at the tourney.

quote: King Aerys was bearded on his coins, the same as old King Aegon. King Daeron, who'd come between them, had been clean-shaved, but this wasn't him.
why: Dunk works out the royal succession -- Aegon, then Daeron, then Aerys -- from the portraits on coinage.

quote: I follow tourneys from afar as faithfully as the maesters follow stars. I know how a certain hedge knight became the cause of a Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow, resulting in the death of Baelor Breakspear at his brother Maekar's hand.
why: Ser Uthor names the Ashford Meadow Trial of Seven and Baelor Breakspear's death at Maekar's hand.

quote: Surely no one hated him that much but Egg's brother Aerion, and the Bright Prince was in exile across the narrow sea.
why: Dunk's private reckoning names Egg's brother Aerion, the Bright Prince, exiled across the narrow sea.

quote: Think on my offer, ser. The snail may leave a trail of slime behind him, but a little slime will do a man no harm ... whilst if you dance with dragons, you must expect to burn.
why: Ser Uthor's veiled warning to Dunk, tying his danger to Targaryen/Blackfyre politics.

quote: After Penny Jenny died, the other whores took care of them and fed the lad the tale his mother had concocted, about him being Fireball's seed.
why: The impeaching backstory of Ser Glendon's claimed Fireball paternity -- a tale his whore mother concocted.

quote: "Half a cup," Underleaf insisted. "You sound in need of it." He filled two cups with wine and handed one to Dunk.
why: Ser Uthor receives Dunk with wine in his tent, just before disclosing the murder contract on Dunk's life.

quote: The ground beneath his feet was carpeted in woven Myrish rugs, rich with color. An ornate trestle table stood surrounded by camp chairs. The feather bed was covered with soft cushions, and an iron brazier burned perfumed incense.
why: The Snail's plain sailcloth tent conceals a merchant's private luxury within.

quote: No. He is Daemon of House Blackfyre, the Second of His Name. Or so he would style himself, if ever he achieves the Iron Throne. You would be surprised to know how many lords prefer their kings brave and stupid.
why: Alyn Cockshaw's in-text reveal that the hedge knight "Ser John the Fiddler" is the Blackfyre pretender Daemon II.

quote: Aye, thought Dunk, and now some fool hedge knight has delivered his favorite son into the hands of his enemies. How better to ensure that the prince never stirs from Summerhall?
why: Dunk's realization that he has unwittingly delivered Prince Maekar's son Egg into the conspiracy's reach.

quote: "Daemon Blackfyre sired seven sons. Two died upon the Redgrass Field, twins. . ."
why: Cockshaw enumerates Daemon Blackfyre's seven sons and the twins lost at the Redgrass Field.

quote: Aerys is weak. A bookish man, and no warrior. The commons hardly know him, and what they know they do not like. His lords love him even less. His father was weak as well, that is true, but when his throne was threatened he had sons to take the field for him. Baelor and Maekar, the hammer and the anvil ... but Baelor Breakspear is no more, and Prince Maekar sulks at Summerhall, at odds with king and Hand.
why: Lord Peake lays out Aerys I's succession weakness now that Baelor Breakspear, one of his two strong sons, is dead.

quote: Many of us had sons and daughters taken to King's Landing to vouchsafe our future loyalty, but most of the hostages perished in the Great Spring Sickness. Our hands are no longer tied. Our time is come.
why: Lord Peake explains that the Great Spring Sickness killed the hostages who had bound the conspirators' loyalty.

quote: And if the dream the prince has dreamed comes true, and a living dragon comes forth here at Whitewalls. . .
why: Lord Peake's speculation that the whole rising pivots on Daemon II's prophetic dragon-hatching dream.

quote: Your sword. I would make you mine own man, and raise you high. My dreams do not lie, Ser Duncan. You will have that white cloak, and I must have the dragon's egg. I must, my dreams have made that plain.
why: The Fiddler's (Daemon II's) recurring claim that his prophetic dreams do not lie.

quote: "No," Lord Peake allowed, "but no man loves a sorcerer, and kinslayers are accursed in the sight of gods and men. At the first sign of weakness or defeat, Bloodraven's men will melt away like summer snows.
why: Lord Peake weighs Bloodraven's standing as a sorcerer and kinslayer as the regime's weak point.

quote: Lord Butterwell wanted a new young wife to warm his bed, and Lord Frey had a somewhat soiled daughter. Their nuptials provided a plausible pretext for some like-minded lords to gather. Most of those invited here fought for the Black Dragon once.
why: The Butterwell-Frey wedding staged as a plausible pretext to gather former Blackfyre men.

quote: His head was broad and bald, his beard black and square. Angry red boils festered on his cheek and neck.
why: Ser Tommard ("Black Tom") Heddle's face revealed once he removes his demonic tourney helm.

quote: Gold inlay made his greaves and gauntlet glitter, and his shield showed nine jade mullets upon a leek-green field. Even his beard was dyed green, in the fashion of the men of Tyrosh across the narrow sea.
why: Ser Galtry the Green's jade-mullet heraldry and Tyroshi green-dyed beard.

quote: Dunk whirled. Through the rain, all he could make out was a hooded shape and a single pale white eye. It was only when the man came forward that the shadowed face beneath the cowl took on the familiar features of Ser Maynard Plumm, the pale eye no more than the moonstone brooch that pinned his cloak at the shoulder.
why: Maynard Plumm's uncanny, briefly one-eyed silhouette resolves into his familiar face and moonstone brooch.

quote: And now they have taken the egg, the dragon's egg my grandsire had from the king himself as a reward for leal service. It was there this morning when I woke, and my guards swear no one entered or left the bedchamber.
why: Butterwell laments the theft of the dragon's egg, a royal gift for his grandsire's loyal service.

quote: He does not bear the sword! If he were his father's son, Bittersteel would have armed him with Blackfyre. And all this talk about a dragon ... madness, madness and folly.
why: Butterwell's doubt about the pretender rests on his not bearing the ancestral sword Blackfyre.

quote: Or else the dragon's hatched, thought Dunk. If a living dragon appeared again in Westeros, the lords and smallfolk alike would flock to whichever prince could lay claim to it.
why: Dunk's realization that a living dragon would be an unstoppable kingmaking prize.

quote: I knew I was in trouble when the maester showed Lord Butterwell my ring. I thought about saying that I'd stolen it, but I didn't think he would believe me. Then I remembered this one time I heard my father talking about something Lord Bloodraven said, about how it was better to be frightening than frightened, so I told them that my father had sent us here to spy for him, that he was on his way here with an army, that His Lordship had best release me and give up this treason, or it would mean his head.
why: Egg talks his way out of danger by invoking Bloodraven's maxim, exposed as Prince Maekar's son by his own ring.

quote: "--is Daemon, aye. They told me. The Black Dragon." Ser Glendon laughed. "My father died for him. I would have been his man, and gladly. I would have fought for him, killed for him, died for him, but I could not lose for him."
why: Ser Glendon's reaction confirms the in-hall reveal that Ser John the Fiddler is Daemon Blackfyre, the pretender.

quote: "A bowl o' brown," they called such fare in the pot shops of King's Landing. Ser Kyle had plainly had no stomach for it. Untouched, the stew had grown cold, and a film of grease glistened atop the brown.
why: The leftover stew ("bowl of brown") served below the salt at the wedding feast, echoing King's Landing pot-shop fare.

quote: The boy had been savagely beaten. His face was bruised and swollen, several of his teeth were cracked or missing, his right eye was weeping blood, and up and down his chest his flesh was red and cracking where they'd burned him with hot irons.
why: Ser Glendon's torture injuries before the trial of arms that will decide his guilt.

quote: "This ... this was ill done," Butterwell finally managed. He turned to Dunk and Egg. "We must be gone from Whitewalls before those two bring word of this to Gormon Peake. He has more friends amongst the guests than I do. The postern gate in the north wall, we'll slip out there ... come, we must make haste."
why: Butterwell abandons his own wedding feast and flees his own castle after the sept killing.

quote: He was a fiddler no more. In place of swords and fiddles, the trapping of his warhorse now displayed the three-headed dragon of House Blackfyre, black on a field of red. The prince had washed the black dye from his hair as well, so it flowed down to his collar in a cascade of silver and gold that glimmered like beaten metal in the torchlight.
why: Daemon II drops his Fiddler disguise entirely, riding out under the Blackfyre dragon with his hair undyed.

quote: The banners of the Prince of Summerhall showed four three-headed dragons, two and two, the arms of the fourth-born son of the late King Daeron II Targaryen. A single white dragon announced the presence of the King's Hand, Lord Brynden Rivers.
why: Bloodraven's own banner (a single white dragon) announces his arrival at Whitewalls with the king's host.

quote: Egg did not flinch. "You know who I am, cousin."
why: Egg confronts Bloodraven and names their kinship on the page -- Prince Aegon, the king's cousin.

quote: And when Daemon drew his sword and raised it above his head, every man of them could see it was not Blackfyre. "We'll make another Redgrass Field today," the pretender promised.
why: Daemon II's drawn sword is visibly not the ancestral Blackfyre, undercutting his claim before the final clash.

quote: "There have always been Targaryens who dreamed of things to come, since long before the Conquest," Bloodraven said, "so we should not be surprised if from time to time a Blackfyre displays the gift as well.
why: Bloodraven states the Targaryen (and by extension Blackfyre) lineage of prophetic dreaming.

quote: Daemon dreamed that a dragon would be born at Whitewalls, and it was. The fool just got the color wrong.
why: Bloodraven confirms Daemon II's dragon-dream was fulfilled -- by Glendon Ball, the "Brown Dragon," not a literal dragon.

quote: Some old dead king gave a sword to one son instead of another, that was the start of it.
why: Dunk's own summation of the Blackfyre wars' root cause: Aegon IV's gift of the sword Blackfyre to Daemon over Daeron.

quote: The First Blackfyre Rebellion had perished on the Redgrass Field in blood and glory. The Second Blackfyre Rebellion ended with a whimper.
why: Narration frames the Whitewalls rising (the Second Blackfyre Rebellion) against the first, on the Redgrass Field.

quote: "That is for King Aerys to decide ... but Daemon has four younger brothers, and sisters as well. Should I be so foolish as to remove his pretty head, his mother will mourn, his friends will curse me for a kinslayer, and Bittersteel will crown his brother Haegon. Dead, young Daemon is a hero.
why: Bloodraven's calculation for sparing Daemon II: killing him only hands Bittersteel a martyr and a fresh pretender in Haegon.

quote: Alive, he is an obstacle in my half brother's path. He can hardly make a third Blackfyre king whilst the second remains so inconveniently alive. Besides, such a noble captive will be an ornament to our court, and a living testament to the mercy and benevolence of His Grace King Aerys.
why: Bloodraven names Bittersteel as his own half brother while acting as Aerys I's Hand.

quote: "Forfeit to the Iron Throne. I mean to pull it down stone by stone and sow the ground that it stands upon with salt. In twenty years, no one will remember it existed.
why: Bloodraven condemns Whitewalls itself to be razed and salted for the treason hosted there.

quote: Old fools and young malcontents still make pilgrimages to the Redgrass Field to plant flowers on the spot where Daemon Blackfyre fell. I will not suffer Whitewalls to become another monument to the Black Dragon.
why: Bloodraven cites the ongoing Blackfyre martyr-cult pilgrimages to the Redgrass Field as reason to raze Whitewalls.

quote: "For a man. A child could do it."
why: Bloodraven's own deduction about how the dragon's egg was stolen -- a clue that the theft, like the tourney's outcome, was never really beyond his sight.
